column2: When choosing among ATX, microATX, and ITX motherboards, what is the main tradeoff a technician is evaluating?
column4: The tradeoff is system size versus expansion room. ATX usually provides the most room and expansion, microATX reduces size with fewer slots, and ITX prioritizes very small systems.
column5:
Why

Form factor affects case fit, mounting, power layout, airflow, and expansion capacity. Exact dimensions are less useful than recognizing which board class fits the scenario.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.34; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic HighYield Motherboard FormFactor Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop build needs a smaller motherboard but still needs broad case and power compatibility. Why might microATX be a reasonable choice?
column4: microATX is smaller than ATX but commonly keeps similar mounting and power compatibility, with the main tradeoff being fewer expansion slots.
column5:
Distinction

microATX is not the smallest option in the objective. It is a middle ground between full-size ATX expansion and very compact ITX systems.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.34; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic Motherboard FormFactor Source::Messer-v170

column2: What type of system is ITX best suited for compared with ATX?
column4: ITX is best suited for compact or small-form-factor systems where low size and limited expansion are acceptable.
column5:
Exam Angle

ITX often appears in scenarios where the system is space-constrained or single-purpose. Do not choose it when the scenario requires many adapter cards.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.34; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic Motherboard FormFactor Source::Messer-v170

column2: How should a technician distinguish PCI from PCIe when selecting an expansion slot?
column4: PCI is an older parallel expansion bus, while PCIe is the newer serial expansion interface used for modern adapter cards and scalable lane widths.
column5:
Distinction

PCIe replaced PCI for most modern expansion-card use. PCIe slots may have different lane sizes, such as x1 or x16, so the card and slot documentation still matters.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.35; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic HighYield Motherboard PCIe ExpansionCards Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is the purpose of motherboard headers during a PC build?
column4: Headers are motherboard pin blocks used for small internal or case wiring connections.
column5:
Why

Headers are easy to confuse with expansion slots. Expansion slots accept add-on cards; headers are smaller motherboard connection points for wiring.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.35; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic HighYield Motherboard Headers Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why might a video card need a power cable in addition to being installed in a PCIe slot?
column4: High-power PCIe cards may need separate 6-pin or 8-pin PCIe power connectors because the slot alone may not provide enough power.
column5:
Scope Boundary

Objective 3.5 includes motherboard and adapter-card power connectors. Overall power-supply selection belongs mainly to Objective 3.6.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.35; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic Motherboard Power PCIe Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is the practical difference between SATA and eSATA motherboard connections?
column4: SATA is used for internal storage connections, while eSATA is the external version used for external SATA storage devices.
column5:
Distinction

Both are storage-related interfaces. The key exam distinction in this objective is internal connector versus external connector.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.26; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic Motherboard SATA Source::Messer-v170

column2: What should a technician verify before installing a device in an M.2 motherboard connector?
column4: Verify that the motherboard supports the device type, keying, size, and interface required by the M.2 device.
column5:
Why

M.2 is a physical connector family, not a guarantee that every M.2 device works in every M.2 slot. The motherboard documentation controls compatibility.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.32; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic Motherboard M2 Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is the first compatibility item to check before installing a CPU in a motherboard?
column4: Check that the motherboard supports the CPU socket and processor family; AMD and Intel CPUs are not interchangeable between socket families.
column5:
Why

Socket compatibility is a hard physical and electrical boundary. Chipset and firmware support can also matter, but the socket is the first practical install check.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.35; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic HighYield Motherboard CPU Compatibility Source::Messer-v170

column2: What does a multisocket motherboard support?
column4: A multisocket motherboard supports more than one physical CPU package on the same motherboard.
column5:
Distinction

Multisocket is not the same as multicore. Multisocket means multiple CPU packages; multicore means multiple cores inside a CPU package.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.35; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic Motherboard CPU Source::Messer-v170

column2: What does changing the boot order in BIOS/UEFI control?
column4: It controls which bootable device the system tries first, and which devices it tries next if the first option does not boot.
column5:
Why

Boot order is a firmware setting. It is commonly changed when installing an operating system, recovering a system, or preventing unauthorized USB boot.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.36; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic BIOS UEFI Boot Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why might an organization disable or restrict USB access in BIOS/UEFI?
column4: To reduce the risk of unauthorized removable storage, data transfer, or booting from external USB devices.
column5:
Why

USB permissions are a firmware-level security control. They can stop access before the operating system loads or before OS policy is applied.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.36; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic BIOS UEFI USB Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is the purpose of Secure Boot in UEFI?
column4: Secure Boot helps ensure that trusted, signed startup software is used during the boot process.
column5:
Exam Trap

Secure Boot is not a boot password. It verifies trust in startup code; a password controls access to startup or firmware settings.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.37; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic HighYield UEFI SecureBoot Security Source::Messer-v170

column2: How does a boot password differ from a BIOS or supervisor password?
column4: A boot password blocks the system from starting until the password is entered, while a BIOS or supervisor password restricts changes to firmware settings.
column5:
Why

Lost firmware passwords may require a motherboard reset procedure, such as a jumper reset, depending on the system. Always check vendor documentation before changing firmware security settings.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.37; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic BIOS UEFI Passwords Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why are temperature monitoring and fan settings commonly found in BIOS/UEFI?
column4: They let the motherboard monitor hardware temperatures and adjust or report fan behavior before relying on operating system software.
column5:
Why

Firmware-level monitoring is useful because the system can check cooling hardware before the OS loads. Exact fan curves and thresholds are vendor-specific.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 pp.36-37; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic BIOS Cooling Fans Source::Messer-v170

column2: A hypervisor cannot use hardware virtualization features even though the CPU supports them. What firmware setting should be checked?
column4: Check whether CPU virtualization support is enabled in BIOS/UEFI.
column5:
Why

The processor may support virtualization, but firmware can disable those features. Hypervisor settings do not replace the need to enable hardware support at the platform level.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.37; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic HighYield BIOS UEFI Virtualization Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is the key difference between a TPM and an HSM?
column4: A TPM provides hardware-backed cryptographic functions for one local device, while an HSM protects cryptographic keys for many systems or services.
column5:
Distinction

TPM is local trust tied to a computer. HSM is centralized or shared key protection, often implemented as a dedicated appliance or high-end security device.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 pp.37-38; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic HighYield TPM HSM Encryption Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why is ARM commonly chosen for mobile, embedded, or IoT-style systems?
column4: ARM is commonly chosen because it can provide efficient processing with lower power use and lower heat output.
column5:
Distinction

The exam contrast is architecture fit, not brand loyalty. ARM is often associated with efficient and compact devices; x86/x64 is common in traditional PC platforms.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.38; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic HighYield CPU Architecture ARM Source::Messer-v170

column2: What does CPU core configuration describe?
column4: It describes how many processing cores are available in the CPU, such as dual-core, quad-core, octa-core, or multicore.
column5:
Distinction

More cores can improve parallel work when software can use them, but core count is not the same thing as multiple physical CPU sockets.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.38; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic CPU Cores Source::Messer-v170

column2: Which expansion card should be installed when a workstation needs to receive video input from an external camera or console?
column4: Install a capture card.
column5:
Distinction

A video card primarily outputs graphics to displays. A capture card is for video input, recording, or streaming from another source.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.38; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic HighYield ExpansionCards CaptureCard Video Source::Messer-v170

column2: When would a technician install a sound card or a NIC instead of relying on motherboard functions?
column4: Install a sound card for additional or higher-quality audio input/output, and install a NIC to add, replace, or expand network connectivity.
column5:
Why

Many motherboards include onboard audio and networking, but expansion cards are still useful when the built-in feature is missing, failed, insufficient, or needs additional ports.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.38; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic ExpansionCards NIC Audio Source::Messer-v170

column2: What should be checked before installing an expansion card?
column4: Check the motherboard slot type, available space, power requirements, operating system support, and driver requirements.
column5:
Why

Expansion-card installation is not just plugging in a board. Documentation helps avoid slot mismatch, blocked adjacent slots, missing auxiliary power, or unsupported drivers.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.39; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic ExpansionCards Drivers Compatibility Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is the purpose of thermal paste or a thermal pad between a CPU and a heat sink?
column4: It improves thermal contact so heat can move efficiently from the CPU into the heat sink.
column5:
Distinction

The heat sink dissipates heat; the paste or pad improves the contact surface between the component and the heat sink. Thermal pads are generally not reusable after removal.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.39; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic HighYield Cooling ThermalPaste ThermalPad Source::Messer-v170

column2: How do fans, heat sinks, and liquid cooling differ in a cooling plan?
column4: Fans move air through or across components, heat sinks conduct heat away from hot parts, and liquid cooling carries heat to a radiator where it can be removed.
column5:
Why

Cooling is a system design issue. Airflow, cable routing, component placement, and heat output all affect whether the selected cooling approach works.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.39; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic Cooling Fans LiquidCooling Source::Messer-v170

column2: When would a technician install a video card?
column4: Install a video card when the system needs dedicated graphics processing or display output beyond what the CPU or motherboard provides.
column5:
Distinction

A video card outputs graphics to displays. A capture card receives video input from an external source.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.38; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.5
column10: A+::220-1201::3.5 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::MotherboardsCPUsandAddonCards Basic ExpansionCards Video Source::Messer-v170
